title: DP on Sequences in Java
summary: Longest Common Subsequence (LCS), Longest Increasing Subsequence (LIS), Edit Distance — 2D DP table intuition and space optimisation.

questions.prompt: A subsequence differs from a substring in that:
choices: It preserves relative order but may skip characters
It must have the same length
It only works on sorted arrays
questions.explanation: 'ACE' is a subsequence of 'ABCDE' (skips B and D). 'ABC' is both a subsequence AND a substring.

questions.prompt: LCS dp[i][j] when s1[i-1] == s2[j-1]:
questions.explanation: Characters match — extend the LCS of both prefixes by 1. The diagonal cell represents the shared common prefix.

questions.prompt: LCS dp[i][j] when s1[i-1] != s2[j-1]:
questions.explanation: Characters don't match — the LCS is the best we can do by skipping one character from either string.

questions.prompt: LCS of 'AB' and 'AB':
questions.code:
// dp table (2x2 + borders)
// chars match at every position
questions.explanation: Both strings are identical — LCS = full length = 2.

questions.prompt: LCS of 'ABC' and 'DEF' is:
questions.explanation: No characters in common — LCS = 0.

questions.prompt: LIS dp[i] (O(n²) approach) is initialised to:
choices: 1 for all i — every element is an LIS of length 1 on its own
questions.explanation: The minimum valid LIS ending at any index is just the element itself — length 1.

questions.prompt: LIS length of [3, 1, 2, 4]:
questions.code:
// LIS could be [1,2,4] or [3,4]
// longest increasing subsequence
questions.explanation: [1,2,4] has length 3. [3,4] has length 2. LIS = 3.

questions.prompt: LIS O(n log n) (patience sorting) replaces the inner O(n) loop with:
choices: Binary search on a 'tails' array to find insertion position
questions.explanation: tails[i] = smallest tail element of all increasing subsequences of length i+1. Binary search finds where to place each element.

questions.prompt: Edit distance base case dp[i][0] = i means:
choices: Cost of converting first i characters of w1 to empty string = delete all i
questions.explanation: Transforming a prefix of length i to an empty string requires i deletions.

questions.prompt: Edit distance when w1[i-1] == w2[j-1]:
questions.explanation: Characters already match — no operation needed. Inherit from the diagonal.

questions.prompt: Edit distance operations mapped to table directions:
choices: Left = insert, Up = delete, Diagonal = replace/match
Left = delete, Up = insert, Diagonal = replace/match
questions.explanation: dp[i][j-1]+1 = insert into w1 (move right in w2). dp[i-1][j]+1 = delete from w1 (move down in w1). dp[i-1][j-1]+1 = replace.

questions.prompt: Edit distance between 'ab' and 'ab':
questions.explanation: Identical strings need 0 operations.

questions.prompt: Edit distance between 'a' and 'b':
questions.explanation: One replace operation transforms 'a' to 'b'.

questions.prompt: Space complexity of 2D DP table for strings of length m and n:
questions.explanation: Full 2D table has (m+1)×(n+1) cells.

questions.prompt: Space-optimised LCS/edit-distance uses two 1D arrays because:
choices: Each cell dp[i][j] only depends on dp[i-1][j-1], dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1] — only the previous row needed
questions.explanation: Previous row = prev[], current row = curr[]. After each outer loop iteration, swap them.

questions.prompt: LCS length also equals:
choices: Number of matching characters at same positions
questions.explanation: This relationship (LCS = (m+n-editDist)/2 when only inserts/deletes) connects the two problems.

questions.prompt: Which are 2D DP problems?
questions.explanation: Coin change is 1D DP (amount is the only state dimension). The others use two state dimensions.

questions.prompt: Shortest Common Supersequence (SCS) length of s1 and s2:
questions.explanation: SCS includes all characters of both strings. Characters in LCS appear once (not twice), so subtract LCS.

questions.prompt: LIS on a strictly decreasing array [5,4,3,2,1]:
questions.explanation: No two elements satisfy nums[i] < nums[j]. Every LIS has length 1.

questions.prompt: To reconstruct the actual LCS string (not just length):
choices: Re-run the algorithm with extra memory
Trace back through the dp table from dp[m][n] to dp[0][0]: diagonal means match, left/up means skip
Sort both strings and find common chars
questions.explanation: Backtrack: if s1[i]==s2[j] → include char, go diagonal. Else go in direction of larger neighbour.

flashcards.back: Subsequence: skip chars, keep order. Substring: contiguous block.
Match: dp[i][j]=1+dp[i-1][j-1]. No match: dp[i][j]=max(dp[i-1][j], dp[i][j-1]).
dp[i]=1 initially. For j<i: if nums[j]<nums[i], dp[i]=max(dp[i], dp[j]+1).
Patience sorting: binary search on tails[] to place each element. Length = tails.size().
Match: dp[i][j]=dp[i-1][j-1]. Else: 1+min(replace diagonal, delete up, insert left).
dp[i][0]=i (delete i chars). dp[0][j]=j (insert j chars).
2D table → two 1D arrays (prev, curr). Swap after each row. O(n) space.
To reconstruct answer: trace from dp[m][n] backward following the recurrence decisions.

project.brief:
Write DPSequencesLab.java with:
1. lcs(String s1, String s2) → int
2. lcsString(String s1, String s2) → String  (reconstruct actual LCS)
3. lis(int[] nums) → int  (O(n²) DP)
4. lisNLogN(int[] nums) → int  (O(n log n) patience sort)
5. editDistance(String w1, String w2) → int
Include main() tests: empty strings, identical strings, fully different strings, decreasing array for LIS.

checklist.text: lcs returns 0 for empty strings or completely different strings
lcsString correctly backtracks through dp table to build the LCS
lis initialises dp[i]=1 for all i before the nested loops
lisNLogN uses Collections.binarySearch returning negative insertion point
editDistance handles empty strings via base case initialisation

hints: lcsString: start at dp[m][n], if chars match add to front of result and go diagonal, else go in direction of larger cell.
lisNLogN: Collections.binarySearch returns -(insertion_point)-1 for missing elements; pos = -(pos+1) converts it.
editDistance: fill dp[i][0]=i and dp[0][j]=j before the main double loop.
